(also known as a **pilot** or a **pilot episode** and sometimes marketed as a **tele-movie**) in [United Kingdom](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom"), [Ireland](/wiki/Television_in_Ireland "Television in Ireland") and [United States television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_States "Television in the United States"), is a standalone [episode](/wiki/Episode "Episode") of a [television series](/wiki/Television_series "Television series") that is used to sell a show to a [television network](/wiki/Television_network "Television network") or other distributor. A pilot is created to be a testing ground to gauge whether a series will be successful. It is, therefore, a test episode for the intended television series, an early step in the series development, much like [pilot studies](/wiki/Pilot_studies "Pilot studies") serve as precursors to the start of larger activity.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Trial episode made to sell a television series

For a list, see [List of television episodes titled Pilot](/wiki/List_of_television_episodes_titled_Pilot "List of television episodes titled Pilot").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Globe icon. | The examples and perspective in this article **deal primarily with the United States and do not represent a [worldwide view](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Countering_systemic_bias "Wikipedia:WikiProject Countering systemic bias") of the subject**. You may [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit), discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Television_pilot "Talk:Television pilot"), or [create a new article](/wiki/Wikipedia:Article_wizard "Wikipedia:Article wizard"), as appropriate. *(September 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A **television pilot** (also known as a **pilot** or a **pilot episode** and sometimes marketed as a **tele-movie**) in [United Kingdom](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom"), [Ireland](/wiki/Television_in_Ireland "Television in Ireland") and [United States television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_States "Television in the United States"), is a standalone [episode](/wiki/Episode "Episode") of a [television series](/wiki/Television_series "Television series") that is used to sell a show to a [television network](/wiki/Television_network "Television network") or other distributor. A pilot is created to be a testing ground to gauge whether a series will be successful. It is, therefore, a test episode for the intended television series, an early step in the series development, much like [pilot studies](/wiki/Pilot_studies "Pilot studies") serve as precursors to the start of larger activity.

A successful pilot may be used as the [series premiere](/wiki/Series_premiere "Series premiere") of a new show, or as a later episode, though some are never aired at all. Some series are commissioned straight-to-series without a pilot (although an increasing number of such series have their first episodes [titled "Pilot"](/wiki/List_of_television_episodes_titled_Pilot "List of television episodes titled Pilot")). On some occasions, pilots that were not ordered to series may also be broadcast as a standalone television film or special.

A "[backdoor pilot](#Backdoor_pilot)" is an episode of an existing series that heavily features supporting characters or guest stars in previously unseen roles. Its purpose is to introduce the characters to an audience before the creators decide on whether or not they intend to pursue a [spin-off](/wiki/Spinoff_(media) "Spinoff (media)") series with those characters.

Television networks use pilots to determine whether an entertaining concept can be successfully realized and whether the expense of additional episodes is justified. *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* estimates that only a little over a quarter of all pilots made for American television proceed to the series stage.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

There have also been cases where features films shown in cinemas like "[M.A.S.H.](/wiki/M.A.S.H. "M.A.S.H.")" and "[Logan's Run](/wiki/Logan%27s_Run "Logan's Run")" inadvertently became "pilots" which inspired later television series.

## Pilot season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Pilot season")]

"Pilot season (television)" redirects here. For the miniseries, see [Pilot Season (TV series)](/wiki/Pilot_Season_(TV_series) "Pilot Season (TV series)").

Each summer, the major [American](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_States "Television in the United States") [broadcast television networks](/wiki/Broadcast_television_networks "Broadcast television networks") – including [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company"), [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW"), [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision"), and [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") – receive about 500 brief [elevator pitches](/wiki/Elevator_pitch "Elevator pitch") each for new shows from writers and producers. That fall, each network requests scripts for about 70 pitches and, the following January, orders about 20 pilot episodes.[[2]](#cite_note-chozick20110512-2) Actors come to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") from within the area or elsewhere in the United States and around the world to [audition](/wiki/Audition "Audition") for them. By spring, actors are cast and production crews assembled to produce the pilots.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Casting is a lengthy and very competitive process. For the 1994 pilot of *[Friends](/wiki/Friends "Friends")*, casting director [Ellie Kanner](/wiki/Ellie_Kanner "Ellie Kanner") reviewed more than 1,000 actors' [head shots](/wiki/Head_shot#Entertainment_industry "Head shot") for each of the six main roles. She summoned 75 actors for each role to audition, and she then chose some to audition again for the show's creators. Of this group, the creators chose some to audition again for [Warner Bros. Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television") executives, who chose the final group of a few actors to audition for NBC executives; as they decide whether to purchase a pilot, network executives generally have ultimate authority over casting.[[4]](#cite_note-kolbert19940406-4) Since the networks work on the same shared schedule, directors, actors and others must choose the best pilot to work for with the hopes that the network will choose it. If it is not chosen, they have wasted their time and money and may have missed out on better career opportunities.[[5]](#cite_note-lots2007-5)

Once they have been produced, the pilots are presented to studio and network executives, and in some cases to test audiences; at this point, each pilot receives various degrees of feedback and is gauged on its potential to advance from one pilot to a full-fledged series. Using this feedback, and factoring in the current status and future potential of their existing series, each network chooses about four to eight pilots for series status.[[2]](#cite_note-chozick20110512-2) The new series are then presented at the networks' annual [upfronts](/wiki/Upfront_(advertising) "Upfront (advertising)") in May, where they are added to network schedules for the following season (either for a fall or "mid-season" winter debut), and at the upfront presentation, the shows are shown to potential advertisers and the networks sell the majority of the advertising for their new pilots.[[5]](#cite_note-lots2007-5) The survival odds for these new series are low, as typically only one or two of them survive for more than one season.[[2]](#cite_note-chozick20110512-2)

## Types of pilots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types of pilots")]

### Premise pilot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Premise pilot")]

A premise pilot introduces the characters and their world to the viewer; it is structured so that it can be run as the first episode of the series if substantial changes are not made between the pilot and [greenlighting](/wiki/Greenlight "Greenlight"). In the event the changes being made are so substantial that they would cause confusion to viewers, the pilot (or portions of it) is often re-shot, recast, or rewritten to fit the rest of the series.

The pilot for *[Gilligan's Island](/wiki/Gilligan%27s_Island "Gilligan's Island")*, for instance, showed the castaways when they had just become stranded on the island. However, three roles were recast before going to series, with the characters either modified or completely altered to the point where the pilot could no longer be used as a regular episode. As a result, [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") aired *Gilligan's* second produced episode, which opened with the same scene of the characters just stranded on the island (showing only those not re-cast), first; the story from the pilot from that point onward was largely reworked into a flashback episode which aired later (with several key scenes re-shot). Even *Gilligan's* theme song, which was originally done as a calypso number, was rewritten and recomposed to be completely different.

Another example is [*Star Trek*](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series "Star Trek: The Original Series"), where footage from the unaired original pilot, "[The Cage](/wiki/The_Cage_(Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series) "The Cage (Star Trek: The Original Series)")", was incorporated into the two-part episode, "[The Menagerie](/wiki/The_Menagerie_(Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series) "The Menagerie (Star Trek: The Original Series)")", with the story justification that it depicts events that happened several years earlier. Conversely, the second pilot for *Star Trek*, "[Where No Man Has Gone Before](/wiki/Where_No_Man_Has_Gone_Before "Where No Man Has Gone Before")", aired as the third episode of the show's first season, even though it included some casting and costuming differences that set it apart from the preceding episodes.

If a network orders a two-hour pilot, it will usually broadcast it as a [television film](/wiki/Television_film "Television film") to recoup some of its costs even if the network chooses to not order the show.[[6]](#cite_note-lowry20000508-6) Sometimes, a made-for-TV-movie is filmed as a pilot, but because of actors not being available, the series intro is reshot for the first aired episode. The original *[Cagney & Lacey](/wiki/Cagney_%26_Lacey "Cagney & Lacey")* movie co-starred [Loretta Swit](/wiki/Loretta_Swit "Loretta Swit") (of *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(TV_series) "M*A*S*H (TV series)")* fame) as Chris Cagney, but when she could not get out of her contract, they reshot it with [Meg Foster](/wiki/Meg_Foster "Meg Foster"), who after the first season was replaced with [Sharon Gless](/wiki/Sharon_Gless "Sharon Gless"); therefore, the original movie is not considered part of the television series, and is not included in the series collections on DVD. In some cases, this does not hamper broadcast, such as [Jackie Cooper](/wiki/Jackie_Cooper "Jackie Cooper") playing the role of Walter Carlson in the TV movie pilot of the 1975 series *[The Invisible Man](/wiki/The_Invisible_Man_(1975_TV_series) "The Invisible Man (1975 TV series)")*, but being replaced by [Craig Stevens](/wiki/Craig_Stevens_(actor) "Craig Stevens (actor)") for the remainder of the series; the pilot is still considered part of the series and released to DVD as such. Likewise, *The Homecoming: A Christmas Story* had an almost entirely different cast from the series it was intended to pilot (*[The Waltons](/wiki/The_Waltons "The Waltons")*), but both have been rerun for many years.

### Proof of concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Proof of concept")]

A [proof of concept](/wiki/Proof_of_concept "Proof of concept") pilot usually takes place chronologically further into a series run than a premise pilot, to give network executives a better feel for how a typical episode would appear (since a premise pilot may have to deviate from a typical episode in order to properly introduce characters). *[Remington Steele](/wiki/Remington_Steele "Remington Steele")* used both a proof of concept and a premise pilot.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Proofs of concept were particularly common for [game shows](/wiki/Game_show "Game show"); in such cases, the pilot may be entirely or partially scripted (and thus, due to regulations passed after the [1950s quiz show scandals](/wiki/1950s_quiz_show_scandals "1950s quiz show scandals"), illegal to broadcast in many jurisdictions) and use fake contestants and "returning champions" to demonstrate those concepts. The adventure series *[Lassie](/wiki/Lassie_(1954_TV_series) "Lassie (1954 TV series)")* had both a premise pilot, "The Inheritance", designed specifically to air as the series' first episode, showing how Lassie's series owner, Jeff Miller, came to acquire her; and a proof of concept pilot, "The Well", showcased situations typical to the series, which aired well on into the first season of the series.

### Backdoor pilot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Backdoor pilot")]

A backdoor pilot is a film or miniseries that serves as a [proof of concept](/wiki/Proof_of_concept "Proof of concept") for a full series,[[9]](#cite_note-9) but may be broadcast on its own even if the full series is not picked up.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The term may also be used for an episode of an existing television show that serves to introduce a potential or already planned [spin-off](/wiki/Spinoff_(media) "Spinoff (media)"). Such backdoor pilots commonly focus on an existing character or characters from the parent series who are to be given their own show.

For example, a 2018 episode of ABC's 1980s-set sitcom *[The Goldbergs](/wiki/The_Goldbergs_(2013_TV_series) "The Goldbergs (2013 TV series)")*, titled "1990-Something", heavily featured teachers who were recurring characters on the series and served as the backdoor pilot to *[Schooled](/wiki/Schooled_(TV_series) "Schooled (TV series)")*, which debuted on January 9, 2019.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Put pilot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Put pilot")]

A put pilot is a pilot that the network has agreed to broadcast either as a special or series; if it does not, it will have to pay substantial monetary penalties to the studio. This usually guarantees that the pilot will be picked up by the network.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Unsold pilot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Unsold pilot")]

An unsold pilot or "busted pilot" is a produced episode that is never broadcast or made into a television series. *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* estimates that only a little over a quarter of all pilots made for American television proceed to the series stage.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

### Test run

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Test run")]

Instead of a single pilot episode, an alternative is a *test run*, a small number of episodes that air as a short-run series with the potential to go into full production if successful. This is particularly common among shows that are intended to be [stripped](/wiki/Strip_programming "Strip programming") (airing five days a week).

Talk shows occasionally use test runs. [Metromedia](/wiki/Metromedia "Metromedia") and its successor [Fox Corporation](/wiki/Fox_Corporation "Fox Corporation") were particularly associated with using test runs for talk shows, with examples including *[The Wendy Williams Show](/wiki/The_Wendy_Williams_Show "The Wendy Williams Show")*,[[14]](#cite_note-14) *The Huckabee Show* (a spin-off of *[Huckabee](/wiki/Huckabee "Huckabee")* that aired for six weeks in summer 2010),[[15]](#cite_note-15) the final version of *[The Jerry Lewis Show](/wiki/The_Jerry_Lewis_Show#Syndicated_version "The Jerry Lewis Show")*,[[16]](#cite_note-16) and *The Kilborn File*, an unsuccessful comeback vehicle for [Craig Kilborn](/wiki/Craig_Kilborn "Craig Kilborn").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2021, [Fox Alternative Entertainment](/wiki/Fox_Alternative_Entertainment "Fox Alternative Entertainment") utilized a [test market](/wiki/Test_market "Test market") approach for its new reality talent competition format *[The Big Deal](/wiki/The_Big_Deal_(2021_TV_series) "The Big Deal (2021 TV series)")*, producing a season of the series for Irish broadcaster [Virgin Media One](/wiki/Virgin_Media_One "Virgin Media One") with the intent to use it as a pitch for Fox and other broadcasters.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### 10/90

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 10/90")]

In a 10/90 production model, a network broadcasts ten episodes of a new television program without ordering a pilot first. If the episodes achieve a predetermined ratings level, the network orders 90 more to bring the total to [100 episodes](/wiki/100_episodes "100 episodes"), immediately enough to [rerun](/wiki/Rerun "Rerun") the show in [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication"). Series that used the 10/90 model include [Tyler Perry](/wiki/Tyler_Perry "Tyler Perry")'s *[House of Payne](/wiki/Tyler_Perry%27s_House_of_Payne "Tyler Perry's House of Payne")*, *[Meet the Browns](/wiki/Meet_the_Browns_(TV_series) "Meet the Browns (TV series)")*, *[For Better or Worse](/wiki/Tyler_Perry%27s_For_Better_or_Worse "Tyler Perry's For Better or Worse")*, [Debmar-Mercury](/wiki/Debmar-Mercury "Debmar-Mercury")'s *[Anger Management](/wiki/Anger_Management_(TV_series) "Anger Management (TV series)")*,[[19]](#cite_note-rose20130116-19) and *[Are We There Yet?](/wiki/Are_We_There_Yet%3F_(TV_series) "Are We There Yet? (TV series)")*. [Byron Allen](/wiki/Byron_Allen "Byron Allen")'s sitcoms followed a similar model, with *[Mr. Box Office](/wiki/Mr._Box_Office "Mr. Box Office")* and *[The First Family](/wiki/The_First_Family_(TV_series) "The First Family (TV series)")* airing 26-episode first seasons with the intention of following them up with a full 104-episode order if successful; both series failed to reach the threshold Allen sought, though they remained in limited production (three to four new episodes a year, mixed in with the first season) for a few years afterward.

#### Other examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Other examples")]

An earlier variant was the 13-episode pilot run; in the late 1980s and early 1990s, [Disney Channel](/wiki/Disney_Channel "Disney Channel") notably gave a 13-episode pilot order to two series it never picked up, but would go on to longer runs on other networks: *[Good Morning, Miss Bliss](/wiki/Good_Morning,_Miss_Bliss "Good Morning, Miss Bliss")* (which also had a traditional pilot on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") and would be revived by that network as *[Saved by the Bell](/wiki/Saved_by_the_Bell "Saved by the Bell")*) and the Canadian drama *Hillside* (which would move to [Nickelodeon](/wiki/Nickelodeon "Nickelodeon"), Disney Channel's primary rival, and air as *[Fifteen](/wiki/Fifteen_(TV_series) "Fifteen (TV series)")*).

## As distinguished from the series premiere

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: As distinguished from the series premiere")]

Main article: [Series premiere](/wiki/Series_premiere "Series premiere")

A successful pilot is often used as the [series premiere](/wiki/Series_premiere "Series premiere"), the first aired episode of a new show, or it may be aired as a later episode or never aired at all. For the Canadian supernatural drama *[Lost Girl](/wiki/Lost_Girl "Lost Girl")*, the pilot that sold the series to [Showcase](/wiki/Showcase_(Canadian_TV_channel) "Showcase (Canadian TV channel)"), "[Vexed](/wiki/List_of_Lost_Girl_episodes#ep8 "List of Lost Girl episodes")", was used as the eighth episode of the first series.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In the case of *[Firefly](/wiki/Firefly_(TV_series) "Firefly (TV series)")*, the original pilot ("Serenity") which was intended to serve as the series premiere was rejected by the network, and a new first episode, "Train Job", was shot specifically for broadcast.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Sometimes, too, viewers will assign the word "pilot" to a work that represented the first appearances of characters and situations later employed by a series – even if the work was not initially intended as a pilot for the series. A good example of this is "Love and the Television Set" (later retitled "Love and the Happy Days" for syndication), an episode of *[Love, American Style](/wiki/Love,_American_Style "Love, American Style")* that featured a version of the Cunningham family. It was in fact a failed pilot for the proposed 1972 series *New Family in Town*, but was recycled as a successful pilot for 1974's *[Happy Days](/wiki/Happy_Days "Happy Days")*.[[22]](#cite_note-22) So firmly embedded is the notion of it as a *Happy Days* pilot, that even series actress [Erin Moran](/wiki/Erin_Moran "Erin Moran") (who did not appear in the episode) viewed it as such, as well as its creator, [Garry Marshall](/wiki/Garry_Marshall "Garry Marshall"), since *Happy Days* itself did not have a separate pilot of its own.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In a similar situation, the 1962 pilot *Howie* was resurrected 13 years later to form the basis of *[The Paul Lynde Show](/wiki/The_Paul_Lynde_Show "The Paul Lynde Show")*.[[24]](#cite_note-filmthreat-24)

The original *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")* TV series had two pilots, neither of which became the premiere episode when the series was picked up. The first, titled *[The Cage](/wiki/The_Cage_(Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series) "The Cage (Star Trek: The Original Series)"),* didn't sell, but [Desilu](/wiki/Desilu "Desilu") head [Lucille Ball](/wiki/Lucille_Ball "Lucille Ball") convinced [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") executives to allow shooting of a second pilot, *[Where No Man Has Gone Before](/wiki/Where_No_Man_Has_Gone_Before "Where No Man Has Gone Before"),* which was accepted by the network. *The Cage* was edited and expanded into a two-part story, shown as *[The Menagerie](/wiki/The_Menagerie_(Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series) "The Menagerie (Star Trek: The Original Series)").* This turned out to be an auspicious decision, because of various challenges which bogged down series production during the first season. The second pilot was also shown during that first season, as the third episode. The only major character to appear in both pilots was [Spock](/wiki/Spock "Spock").

On other occasions, the pilot is never broadcast on television at all. Viewers of *[Temple Houston](/wiki/Temple_Houston_(TV_series) "Temple Houston (TV series)")*, for example, would likely have considered "The Twisted Rope" its pilot because "The Man from Galveston" was only publicly exhibited in cinemas four months later. Even then, "The Man from Galveston" had an almost entirely different cast, and its main character was renamed to avoid confusion with the then-ongoing series.

Some television series are commissioned "straight-to-series" where a network orders a season without viewing any produced episodes, hence no episode is considered a pilot. For instance, "[Invasion of the Bane](/wiki/Invasion_of_the_Bane "Invasion of the Bane")", the first episode of *[The Sarah Jane Adventures](/wiki/The_Sarah_Jane_Adventures "The Sarah Jane Adventures")*, is not a pilot because the [BBC](/wiki/British_Broadcasting_Corporation "British Broadcasting Corporation") had committed to the first season before seeing any filmed content[[25]](#cite_note-25) – yet it is routinely referred to as a pilot.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) The straight-to-series model is usually used when established talent is attached to a series, or it is based on an established property or franchise. [*Amazing Stories* (1985)](/wiki/Amazing_Stories_(1985_TV_series) "Amazing Stories (1985 TV series)") is credited as being one of the first series commissioned without a pilot. The model has seen a rise since [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") popularized it.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Theatrical release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Theatrical release")]

A number of unsold pilots have been reworked into theatrically released feature films, including *[Lum and Abner Abroad](/wiki/Lum_and_Abner_Abroad "Lum and Abner Abroad")* (1956), which wove together three pilot episodes for a 1956 series that would have starred the comedy duo of [Lum and Abner](/wiki/Lum_and_Abner "Lum and Abner"); *[Agent for H.A.R.M.](/wiki/Agent_for_H.A.R.M. "Agent for H.A.R.M.")* (1966); and *[Mulholland Drive](/wiki/Mulholland_Drive_(film) "Mulholland Drive (film)")* (2001), which was composed of an unsold pilot episode appended with an ending shot specifically for the film. The 1966 film release, *[Tarzan and the Trappers](/wiki/Tarzan_and_the_Trappers "Tarzan and the Trappers")* was edited from the unbroadcast pilot of a proposed 1958 *Tarzan* series.

In addition, a number of unsuccessful pilot episodes have been released as [direct-to-video](/wiki/Direct-to-video "Direct-to-video") films, including *[Belle's Magical World](/wiki/Belle%27s_Magical_World "Belle's Magical World")* (1998), *[Cruel Intentions 2](/wiki/Cruel_Intentions_2 "Cruel Intentions 2")* (2001) and *[Atlantis: Milo's Return](/wiki/Atlantis:_Milo%27s_Return "Atlantis: Milo's Return")* (2003).

On a few occasions, pilots have been released as a theatrical films prior to the debuts of their respective series. Examples include *[Battlestar Galactica](/wiki/Battlestar_Galactica_(1978_TV_series) "Battlestar Galactica (1978 TV series)")*, whose pilot was theatrically released during the summer of 1978, prior to its broadcast as the opening episodes of the TV series that fall, and 1979's *[Buck Rogers in the 25th Century](/wiki/Buck_Rogers_in_the_25th_Century_(film) "Buck Rogers in the 25th Century (film)")*, released in early 1979, with the series launching in the fall. In both cases there are substantial differences between theatrical and televised version: both BSG and Buck Rogers' theatrical pilots had recurring characters (Baltar and Tiger Man, respectively) killed off, the BSG pilot was extended for television, and the televised version of the *Buck Rogers* pilot utilized a different opening credits sequence and featured a newly added epilogue scene intended to lead into the weekly series.

## Use in anthology series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Television_pilot&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Use in anthology series")]

By the mid-1950s, the practice of television executives of ordering dozens of pilots for proposed television series each year — far more than their networks could possibly broadcast as series — had created a sizable body of unsold pilots that had never aired.[[29]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19561967-29) By 1954, the American [television industry](/wiki/Television_industry "Television industry") had begun to consider the idea of packaging these unsold pilots in [anthology series](/wiki/Anthology_series "Anthology series") and airing them during the summer, providing television networks with a way of both providing fresh programming during the summer rerun season and recouping at least some of the expense of producing them.[[29]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19561967-29)

On June 8, 1956, the *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")* reported that the [American Broadcasting Company](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") (ABC) would begin airing a package of unsold pilots that summer under the title *[G.E. Summer Originals](/wiki/G.E._Summer_Originals "G.E. Summer Originals")*, adding that "the problem of what to do with 'pilot' or sample films of projected television series that previously have failed to sell has been solved."[[29]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19561967-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) *G.E. Summer Originals* premiered on the evening of July 3, 1956, at exactly the same time as another anthology series of unsold pilots, *[Sneak Preview](/wiki/Sneak_Preview_(TV_series) "Sneak Preview (TV series)")* on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), and these thus became the first two series of unsold pilots to air in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[29]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19561967-29)

A number of summer anthology series consisting entirely or partly of unsold pilots were broadcast in the United States between 1956 and 1989. These series were:[[29]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19561967-29)[[31]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19671989-31)

* *[G.E. Summer Originals](/wiki/G.E._Summer_Originals "G.E. Summer Originals")* (ABC, 1956)
* *[Sneak Preview](/wiki/Sneak_Preview_(TV_series) "Sneak Preview (TV series)")* (NBC, 1956)
* *[Colgate Theatre](/wiki/Colgate_Theatre_(1958_TV_series) "Colgate Theatre (1958 TV series)")* (NBC, 1958)
* *[Decision](/wiki/Decision_(TV_series) "Decision (TV series)")* (NBC, 1958)
* *[New Comedy Showcase](/wiki/New_Comedy_Showcase "New Comedy Showcase")* ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), 1960)
* *[The Comedy Spot](/wiki/The_Comedy_Spot "The Comedy Spot")* (CBS, 1960 and 1962)
* *[Westinghouse Preview Theatre](/wiki/Westinghouse_Preview_Theatre "Westinghouse Preview Theatre")* (CBS, 1961)
* *[Vacation Playhouse](/wiki/Vacation_Playhouse "Vacation Playhouse")* (CBS, 1963–1967)
* *[Summer Playhouse](/wiki/Summer_Playhouse_(1964_TV_series) "Summer Playhouse (1964 TV series)")* (CBS, 1964–1965)
* *[Preview Tonight](/wiki/Preview_Tonight "Preview Tonight")* (ABC, 1966)
* *[Summer Fun](/wiki/Summer_Fun_(TV_series) "Summer Fun (TV series)")* (ABC, 1966)
* *[Premiere](/wiki/Premiere_(TV_series) "Premiere (TV series)")* (CBS, 1968)
* *[Comedy Playhouse](/wiki/Comedy_Playhouse_(American_TV_series) "Comedy Playhouse (American TV series)")* (CBS, 1971)
* *[Just for Laughs](/wiki/Just_for_Laughs_(1974_TV_series) "Just for Laughs (1974 TV series)")* (ABC, 1974)
* *[Comedy Theatre](/wiki/Comedy_Theatre_(TV_series) "Comedy Theatre (TV series)")* (NBC, 1976 and 1979)
* *[Comedy Time](/wiki/Comedy_Time "Comedy Time")* (NBC, 1977)
* *[Comedy Theater](/wiki/Comedy_Theater "Comedy Theater")* (NBC, 1981)
* *[CBS Summer Playhouse](/wiki/CBS_Summer_Playhouse "CBS Summer Playhouse")* (CBS, 1987–1989)

By the mid-1980s, the rise of [cable television](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") outlets had led to an increase of original programming during the summer months and ABC, CBS, and NBC began to experience a decline in summer viewership;[[31]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19671989-31) the launch of [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") as a fourth major network in 1987 only exacerbated the problem for the former "Big Three" networks.[[31]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19671989-31) Although CBS viewed *CBS Summer Playouse* — broadcast during the summers of 1987, 1988, and 1989 — as original programming that addressed this issue, it was the last anthology series of unsold pilots.[[31]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19671989-31) Unsold pilots aired as one-offs occasionally during the 1990s, but then the practice of broadcasting them ended almost entirely.[[31]](#cite_note-tvobsecurities19671989-31)
